64	# Blueprint Writer
65

109	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
110

112	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
113	  \label{thm:some_label}
114	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
115	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}

117	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
118	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
119	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
121	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}

127	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."

135	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149

151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
153	
154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.

158	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
159	
160	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
165	
166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.

171	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
172	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
173	- **Add, correct, or complete `\uses{...}` cross-references** so the dependency edges match the real mathematics. If your chapter's proof of `T` invokes lemma `L`, `T` must declare `\uses{lem:L}`; if a `\uses{}` points at a label that no longer exists, fix it. "It's trivial" is not a reason to skip it: the entry is what carries the dependency edges and what helps a later prover fill the sorry. 205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.

242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:

251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.

102	/-! ## §1. The Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve -/
103	
104	/-- **Euler characteristic of a `ModuleCat k`-valued sheaf on a smooth proper
105	curve `C / k̄`.**
106	
107	On a curve (one-dimensional scheme), Grothendieck vanishing
108	(Hartshorne III.2.7) gives `H^i(C, 𝓕) = 0` for `i ≥ 2`, so the classical
109	alternating sum
110	`χ(𝓕) = Σ_{i ≥ 0} (-1)^i dim_{k̄} H^i(C, 𝓕)` collapses to the two-term
111	expression
112	`χ(𝓕) = dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝓕) − dim_{k̄} H¹(C, 𝓕)`.
113	
114	This is the definition we ship. Coherence of `𝓕` on the proper `k̄`-scheme
115	`C` guarantees that both `H⁰` and `H¹` are finite-dimensional `k̄`-vector
116	spaces (Serre's coherent-cohomology finiteness theorem, the same
117	finiteness backing `AlgebraicGeometry.genus`), so the two `Module.finrank`s
118	are honest natural numbers and the difference is a well-defined integer.
119	
120	The `Module k̄`-valued cohomology pipeline is the project's
121	`Scheme.HModule` (iter-009), the same wrapper used by
122	`AlgebraicGeometry.genus`.
123	
124	Blueprint reference: `def:eulerChar_curve`
125	(Hartshorne IV.1 p. 295, displayed inside the proof of Theorem 1.3). -/
126	noncomputable def Scheme.eulerCharacteristic
127	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
128	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
129	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
130	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
131	    (F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
132	      (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) : ℤ :=
133	  (Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar F 0) : ℤ)
134	    - (Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar F 1) : ℤ)
135	
136	/-! ## §2. The invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(D)` of a Weil divisor (sibling chapter)
137	
138	The honest construction of `𝒪_C(D)` lives in the sibling chapter `RR.3`
139	(`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`, `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean`),
140	where the locally-principal ideal sheaves of closed points are glued
141	into an invertible `𝒪_C`-module. **iter-183 Lane K** opened that file
142	with the typed-`sorry` pin `Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf` plus the three
143	immediate corollaries `sheafOf_zero`, `sheafOf_singlePoint`, and
144	`sheafOf_ses_single_add`. **iter-183 Lane H** (this file) retires the
145	former local `sheafOf` typed-`sorry` placeholder (previously at L168 in
146	the iter-174 skeleton) by importing `OcOfD.lean`; downstream pins (the
147	`ℓ`-invariant, the χ-identity, the genus-`0` Riemann–Roch formula) now
148	reference the chapter's canonical `sheafOf` directly. -/
149	
150	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
151	
152	variable {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
153	  {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsProper C.hom]
154	  [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
155	  [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
156	
157	/-! ## §3. The `ℓ`-invariant of a Weil divisor -/
158	
159	/-- **The `ℓ`-invariant of a Weil divisor `D` on a smooth proper curve
160	`C / k̄`.**
161	
162	By definition,
163	`ℓ(D) := dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(D)) ∈ ℕ`,
164	the `k̄`-dimension of the space of global sections of the invertible sheaf
165	`𝒪_C(D)` associated to `D`. Finiteness of `ℓ(D)` is a consequence of the
166	coherent-cohomology finiteness theorem on a proper `k̄`-scheme
167	(Hartshorne II.5.19 / III.5.2), the same input that backs the finiteness
168	of `genus C`.
169	
170	The classical *complete linear system* `|D|` has `k̄`-projective dimension
171	`ℓ(D) − 1`; the chapter never uses this projective interpretation —
172	`ℓ(D)` is the only quantity consumed by the Hartshorne IV.1.3.5 chain.
173	
174	Blueprint reference: `def:l_invariant` (Hartshorne IV.1 p. 295,
175	"We denote `dim_k H⁰(X, 𝒪(D))` by `l(D)`"). -/
176	noncomputable def l (D : C.left.WeilDivisor) : ℕ :=
177	  Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar (sheafOf (C := C) D) 0)
178	
179	end Scheme.WeilDivisor

181	/-! ## §4. The χ-identity: `χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g`
182	
183	The bridge from the structural Euler characteristic to the arithmetic
184	degree of a divisor. Hartshorne IV.1 Theorem 1.3 reduces Riemann–Roch to
185	this identity; the proof is the inductive `D ↔ D + [P]` step (additivity
186	of χ on the closed-point short exact sequence, with base case `D = 0`
187	giving `χ(𝒪_C) = 1 − g` from `dim H⁰(C, 𝒪_C) = 1` and the definition of
188	the genus). The closure of the body is iter-175+ work after the
189	`Euler-characteristic additivity on a short exact sequence`-style
190	project-side helper is supplied and the `RR.3` sheaf `𝒪_C(D)` has a
191	body.
192	
193	**Iter-181 Lane H factoring.** Both Hartshorne IV.1.3 inputs (the base
194	case `χ(𝒪_C) = 1 − g` and the inductive step `χ(𝒪_C(D + Y)) = χ(𝒪_C(D))
195	+ n`) intrinsically reference the body of `Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf`,
196	which is still a typed-`sorry` placeholder waiting on the sibling
197	chapter `RR.3` (`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`). We therefore factor the proof
198	into two named substantive helper lemmas (3-tier disclosure: **honest
199	named-sorry helpers** — each helper has a substantive type encoding a
200	nontrivial mathematical claim about the `sheafOf` line bundle whose
201	closure is downstream of the `RR.3` body):
202	
203	1. `eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_zero` — base case `χ(sheafOf 0) = 1 − g`,
204	   which on closure of `RR.3` reduces to `sheafOf 0 = toModuleKSheaf C`
205	   plus the Hartshorne I.3.4 input `dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C) = 1` plus the
206	   definition of the genus.
207	2. `eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_single_add` — inductive step
208	   `χ(sheafOf (Finsupp.single Y n + D)) = χ(sheafOf D) + n` for any
209	   `Y : C.left.PrimeDivisor` and `n : ℤ`. On closure of `RR.3` this
210	   reduces to the Hartshorne IV.1.3 SES additivity argument iterated
211	   `|n|` times (with sign for `n < 0`).
212	
213	The main theorem then closes by induction on the `Finsupp`-structure of
214	`D : C.left.WeilDivisor` (via `Finsupp.induction`), using the two
215	helpers and the additivity of `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree`. -/
216	
217	/-- **Hartshorne I.3.4 bridge** (Lane H helper A — iter-183).
218	On a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄`, the global
219	sections of the structure sheaf form a one-dimensional `k̄`-vector space:
220	`dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C) = 1`.
221	
222	This is the Hartshorne~I.3.4 statement "for any projective variety `X`
223	over an algebraically closed field `k`, `H⁰(X, 𝒪_X) ≅ k`" specialised to
224	the project's `ModuleCat k̄`-flavoured cohomology pipeline
225	(`Scheme.HModule kbar (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C) 0`). The closure lives
226	in the project's `Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK` chapter (the H⁰
227	bridge from the constant-sheaf adjunction) and is gated on the
228	remaining cohomology-API work in that file.
229	
230	**iter-183 Lane H status** — Tier-3 honest typed sorry. Body iter-184+
231	via the `Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK` H⁰-bridge. -/
232	private theorem Scheme.finrank_H0_toModuleKSheaf_eq_one
233	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
234	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
235	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
236	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left] :
237	    Module.finrank kbar
238	        (Scheme.HModule kbar (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C) 0) = 1 := by
239	  -- Step 1: H⁰-bridge LinearEquiv chain (existing project infrastructure).
240	  -- `HModule kbar (toModuleKSheaf C) 0` is identified, step by step, with
241	  -- the underlying global-sections ring `C.left.presheaf.obj (op ⊤)` via:
242	  --   `HModule_zero_linearEquiv`         -- Ext₀ ≃ Hom from constant sheaf;
243	  --   `constantSheafGammaHom_linearEquiv` -- constant-sheaf-Γ adjunction;
244	  --   `homFromOne_linearEquiv`            -- Hom-from-`k` evaluation at `1`;
245	  --   `SheafGammaObj_linearEquiv_top`     -- `Sheaf.Γ` reads the top section.
246	  let LE1 :=
247	    Scheme.HModule_zero_linearEquiv kbar (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C)
248	  let LE2 :=
249	    AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.constantSheafGammaHom_linearEquiv kbar
250	      (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat) (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)
251	      (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C)
252	  let LE3 :=
253	    AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.homFromOne_linearEquiv kbar
254	      ((Sheaf.Γ (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
255	        (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)).obj (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C))
256	  let LE4 :=
257	    AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.SheafGammaObj_linearEquiv_top kbar
258	      (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C)
259	  let LE := ((LE1.trans LE2).trans LE3).trans LE4
260	  rw [LE.finrank_eq]
261	  -- Step 2: `(toModuleKSheaf C).obj.obj (op ⊤)` is definitionally
262	  -- `ModuleCat.of kbar (C.left.presheaf.obj (op ⊤))` (per `toModuleKPresheaf_obj`),
263	  -- so we transport to the global-sections ring and apply
264	  -- `Module.finrank_of_bijective_algebraMap` + `IsAlgClosed.algebraMap_bijective_of_isIntegral`.
265	  -- Convert the ModuleCat coercion to the underlying global sections ring.
266	  change Module.finrank kbar

645	      rw [← add_assoc, ← Finsupp.single_add]
646	      congr 2
647	      ring
648	    have hpred := Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_succ
649	      C (Finsupp.single Y (-(k : ℤ) - 1) + D) Y
650	    -- hpred: χ(sheafOf (single Y 1 + (single Y (-k-1) + D))) = χ(sheafOf (single Y (-k-1) + D)) + 1
651	    -- Combine via hsplit and congr 1 to bridge dot-notation vs full-name display:
652	    have key : Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C
653	        (Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf (C := C) (Finsupp.single Y (-(k : ℤ)) + D))
654	        = Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C
655	            (Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf (C := C)
656	              (Finsupp.single Y 1 + (Finsupp.single Y (-(k : ℤ) - 1) + D))) :=
657	      congrArg (fun e => Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C
658	        (Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf (C := C) e)) hsplit
659	    -- ih: χ(sheafOf (single Y (-k) + D)) = χ(sheafOf D) + (-k)
660	    -- key: χ(sheafOf (single Y (-k) + D)) = χ(sheafOf (single Y 1 + (single Y (-k - 1) + D)))
661	    -- hpred: χ(sheafOf (s Y 1 + (s Y (-k-1) + D))) = χ(sheafOf (s Y (-k-1) + D)) + 1
662	    -- Goal: χ(sheafOf (single Y (-k - 1) + D)) = χ(sheafOf D) + (-k - 1)
663	    linarith [key, hpred, ih]
664	
665	/-- **Euler-characteristic identity for `𝒪_C(D)` on a smooth proper curve
666	of genus `g`.**
667	
668	For every Weil divisor `D ∈ Div(C)`,
669	`χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g(C)`.
670	
671	The proof is Hartshorne IV.1 Theorem 1.3's reduction: induction on the
672	free-abelian-group structure of `Div(C)` on closed points, base case `D =
673	0` giving `χ(𝒪_C) = 1 − g` (the `dim H⁰(C, 𝒪_C) = 1` is the Hartshorne
674	I.3.4 input via the project's `H⁰`-bridge), and inductive step via
675	additivity of χ on the closed-point short exact sequence
676	`0 → 𝒪_C(D) → 𝒪_C(D + [P]) → k(P) → 0`.
677	
678	Blueprint reference: `thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus`
679	(Hartshorne IV.1 Theorem 1.3, p. 295).
680	
681	**Iter-181 Lane H status** — the main theorem is closed by induction on
682	the `Finsupp` structure of `D`, consuming the two `sheafOf`-side helper
683	sorries `eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_zero` (base case) and
684	`eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_single_add` (inductive step). The body
685	itself is honest; its `sorryAx`-transitivity is the inevitable
686	consequence of `sheafOf` still being a typed sorry waiting on `RR.3`. -/
687	theorem Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus
688	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
689	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
690	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
691	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
692	    (D : C.left.WeilDivisor) :
693	    Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C (Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf (C := C) D)
694	      = (Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree D) + 1 - (AlgebraicGeometry.genus C : ℤ) := by
695	  -- Unfold `WeilDivisor` to expose the underlying `Finsupp` structure so
696	  -- `Finsupp.induction` applies directly.
697	  unfold Scheme.WeilDivisor at D
698	  induction D using Finsupp.induction with
699	  | zero =>
700	    -- D = 0: χ(sheafOf 0) = 1 - g by helper 1, and degree 0 = 0.
701	    exact (Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_zero C).trans
702	      (by simp [Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree])
703	  | single_add Y n D' _hY _hn hD' =>
704	    -- D = single Y n + D': use helper 2 then the inductive hypothesis.
705	    rw [Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_single_add, hD']
706	    -- Goal: (degree D' + 1 - g) + n = degree (single Y n + D') + 1 - g.
707	    have hdeg : Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree
708	        ((Finsupp.single Y n + D' : C.left.WeilDivisor))
709	        = n + Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree D' := by
710	      change ((Finsupp.single Y n + D' : C.left.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ)).sum
711	          (fun _ z => z) = _
712	      classical
713	      rw [Finsupp.sum_add_index (fun _ _ => rfl) (fun _ _ _ _ => rfl)]
714	      simp [Finsupp.sum_single_index]
715	      rfl
716	    linarith
717	
718	/-! ## §5. The Riemann–Roch formula in genus zero -/
719	
720	/-- **Riemann–Roch in genus zero (Hartshorne IV.1 Example 1.3.5).**
721	
722	Let `C` be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over the
723	algebraically closed field `k̄` with `g(C) = 0`, and let `D ∈ Div(C)` with
724	`deg D ≥ 0`. Then
725	`ℓ(D) = deg(D) + 1`.
726	
727	The proof specialises the χ-identity
728	`eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus` to `g = 0`,
729	unfolds `χ` via `def:eulerChar_curve`, and absorbs the `H¹`-vanishing
730	hypothesis (named premise `_hH1`: `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(D)) = 0`, which is the
731	`H¹`-vanishing of a non-negative-degree invertible sheaf on a
732	genus-`0` curve, to be discharged by `RR.3` once `𝒪_C(D)` has a body and
733	the cohomology of `𝒪_{ℙ¹}(d)` is computed).
734	
735	Blueprint reference: `thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero` (Hartshorne IV.1
736	Example 1.3.5, p. 297). -/
737	theorem Scheme.WeilDivisor.l_eq_degree_plus_one_of_genus_zero
738	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
739	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
740	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
741	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
742	    (D : C.left.WeilDivisor) (_hg : AlgebraicGeometry.genus C = 0)
743	    (_hdeg : (0 : ℤ) ≤ Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree D)
744	    (_hH1 : Module.finrank kbar
745	      (Scheme.HModule kbar (Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf (C := C) D) 1) = 0) :
746	    (Scheme.WeilDivisor.l (C := C) D : ℤ)
747	      = Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree D + 1 := by
748	  have h := Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus C D
749	  simp only [Scheme.eulerCharacteristic, _hg, _hH1,
750	    Nat.cast_zero, sub_zero] at h
751	  exact h
752	
753	end AlgebraicGeometry

85	\section{The Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve}
86	
87	\begin{definition}
88	
89	
90	\leanok
91	[Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve]
92	  \label{def:eulerChar_curve}
93	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic}
94	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule}
95	
96	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(\chi(\mathcal F)\)
97	  % inside the proof of Theorem~1.3) (read from
98	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
99	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
100	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
101	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
102	  % is the Euler characteristic
103	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
104	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295, the formula displayed
105	  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3.}
106	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let \(\mathcal F\)
107	  be a coherent sheaf on \(C\). The \emph{Euler characteristic} of
108	  \(\mathcal F\) is the integer
109	  \[
110	    \chi(\mathcal F) \;:=\;
111	    \sum_{i \geq 0} (-1)^i \,\dim_{\bar k} H^i(C, \mathcal F)
112	    \;=\;
113	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal F)
114	    \,-\,
115	    \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal F).
116	  \]
117	  The second equality is a curve-specific simplification: because
118	  \(\dim C = 1\), Grothendieck vanishing (Hartshorne~III.2.7) gives
119	  \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i \geq 2\), so the alternating sum has
120	  only two terms. Coherence of \(\mathcal F\) on the proper $\bar
121	  k\(-scheme \)C\( guarantees that both \)H^0\( and \)H^1$ are
122	  finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector spaces (Serre's coherent-cohomology
123	  finiteness theorem, the same finiteness backing
124	  REF), so the two terms are honest natural numbers and
125	  the difference is a well-defined integer.
126	
127	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
128	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic} takes a coherent
129	  sheaf \(\mathcal F\) on \(C\) and returns an integer; the natural input
130	  shape is the project's \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf-cohomology
131	  wrapper \(\HModule \bar k\) (REF) applied to a
132	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured carrier built from \(\mathcal F\), with
133	  \(\dim_{\bar k}\) extracted via \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\). The
134	  declaration is \texttt{noncomputable} (inherited from
135	  \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\) and from the \(\Ext\)-based cohomology
136	  construction).
137	\end{definition}

139	\section{The \(\ell\)-invariant of a Weil divisor}
140	
141	\begin{definition}
142	
143	
144	\leanok
145	[\(\ell\)-invariant of a Weil divisor]
146	  \label{def:l_invariant}
147	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l}
148	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule, def:codim1_cycles, def:sheafOf}
149	
150	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(l(D)\) and of the
151	  % "dimension of \(|D|\)") (read from
152	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
153	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We denote \(\dim_k H^0(X, \mathscr L(D))\) by \(l(D)\),
154	  % so that the \emph{dimension of \(|D|\)} is \(l(D) - 1\). The number
155	  % \(l(D)\) is finite by (II, 5.19) or (III, 5.2)."
156	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(l(D)\)).}
157	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let
158	  \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) be a Weil divisor (REF). The
159	  \emph{\(\ell\)-invariant of \(D\)} is the integer
160	  \[
161	    \ell(D) \;:=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))
162	    \;\in\; \mathbb N,
163	  \]
164	  the \(\bar k\)-dimension of the space of global sections of the
165	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) associated to \(D\). Finiteness of
166	  \(\ell(D)\) is a consequence of the coherent-cohomology finiteness
167	  theorem on a proper \(\bar k\)-scheme (Hartshorne~II.5.19 or
168	  III.5.2). The classical \emph{complete linear system \(|D|\)} has
169	  \(\bar k\)-dimension \(\ell(D) - 1\) as a projective space, but the
170	  chapter never uses the projective-space interpretation; \(\ell(D)\) is
171	  the only quantity it needs.
172	
173	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
174	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l} accepts a divisor
175	  \(D : C.\mathtt{WeilDivisor}\) (REF) and returns
176	  \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\;\bar k\;(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D)))\),
177	  computed through the same \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf-cohomology
178	  pipeline as REF. The associated invertible
179	  sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) is the line bundle of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling
180	  chapter, to be added); the present chapter's prose and Lean
181	  signature reference \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) by name and consume only its
182	  \(H^0\) functor. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
183	\end{definition}

185	\section{The \(\chi\) identity: \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\)}
186	
187	\subsection*{Substrate lemmas for the proof of the \(\chi\)-identity}
188	
189	The proof of REF below
190	decomposes into three substantive substrate lemmas, factored out of the
191	Hartshorne~IV.1.3 inductive argument. The first
192	(REF, \emph{additivity}) is the
193	classical statement that \(\chi\) descends to the Grothendieck group
194	of \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\); the second
195	(REF, \emph{iso-invariance}) is the
196	elementary fact that \(\chi\) is well defined on isomorphism classes
197	of sheaves; the third
198	(REF, \emph{skyscraper}) computes \(\chi\)
199	on a closed-point skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\). Pinning each as its own
200	Lean declaration lets the prover attack the three substrate gaps
201	independently of the parent inductive argument.
202	
203	\begin{lemma}\leanok
204	[Additivity of the Euler characteristic on a short exact sequence]
205	  \label{lem:euler_char_shortExact_add}
206	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add}
207	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, def:Scheme_HModule}
208	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (inside the proof of Theorem~1.3,
209	  % the citation ``III, Ex.~5.1'' for the additivity of \(\chi\) on a
210	  % short exact sequence) (read from
211	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
212	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
213	  % exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1), and \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), so we have
214	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\)."
215	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1 p.~296 (citation of III, Ex.~5.1
216	  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3).}
217	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
218	  \(\bar k\), and let
219	  \[
220	    0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0
221	  \]
222	  be a short exact sequence in the category of \(\Module \bar k\)-valued
223	  sheaves on \(C\). Then
224	  \[
225	    \chi(\mathcal F_2) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal F_1) + \chi(\mathcal F_3).
226	  \]
227	\end{lemma}
228	
229	\begin{proof}
230	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
231	  Apply the project-side \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured covariant Ext-LES
232	  (Mathlib's \texttt{Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence}) to the constant-sheaf
233	  test object \(L = (\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;
234	  \bar k\;\bar k)\) and the short exact sequence
235	  \(0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0\). The
236	  resulting nine-term sequence
237	  \[
238	    H^0(\mathcal F_1) \to H^0(\mathcal F_2) \to H^0(\mathcal F_3)
239	    \to H^1(\mathcal F_1) \to H^1(\mathcal F_2) \to H^1(\mathcal F_3)
240	    \to H^2(\mathcal F_1) \to H^2(\mathcal F_2) \to H^2(\mathcal F_3)
241	  \]
242	  is exact in the category of \(\bar k\)-vector spaces. Grothendieck
243	  vanishing \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i \geq 2\) on the
244	  one-dimensional scheme \(C\) (Hartshorne~III.2.7) truncates the
245	  sequence to a six-term exact sequence
246	  \[
247	    0 \to K_0 \to H^0(\mathcal F_1) \to H^0(\mathcal F_2) \to
248	    H^0(\mathcal F_3) \to H^1(\mathcal F_1) \to H^1(\mathcal F_2)
249	    \to H^1(\mathcal F_3) \to 0
250	  \]
251	  (with \(K_0 = 0\) the kernel of the first map, kept here only as a
252	  bookkeeping placeholder). Rank-counting on a six-term exact sequence
253	  of finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector spaces — iterated applications
254	  of \(\mathtt{Submodule.finrank\_quotient\_add\_finrank}\) at each
255	  intermediate kernel/image quotient — yields the alternating identity
256	  \[
257	    \dim H^0(\mathcal F_1) - \dim H^0(\mathcal F_2) + \dim H^0(\mathcal F_3)
258	    - \dim H^1(\mathcal F_1) + \dim H^1(\mathcal F_2) - \dim H^1(\mathcal F_3)
259	    \;=\; 0,
260	  \]
261	  which rearranges, using the definition
262	  \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\)
263	  (REF), to
264	  \(\chi(\mathcal F_2) = \chi(\mathcal F_1) + \chi(\mathcal F_3)\).
265	
266	  % NOTE: gated on project-side LES carrier + finiteness machinery.
267	  % Mathlib `b80f227` ships `Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence` but not (a)
268	  % the `ModuleCat kbar`-flavoured LES carrier specialised to the
269	  % project's sheaf-cohomology pipeline `Scheme.HModule`, (b) Grothendieck
270	  % vanishing `H^i(C, F) = 0` for `i ≥ 2` at the `Ext`-cohomology level
271	  % consumed by `HModule`, nor (c) the six-term rank-counting bridge
272	  % composed of `Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank` invocations.
273	  % The Lean helper `eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add` therefore
274	  % carries a Tier-3 honest typed sorry; its closure lands once the
275	  % three substrate pieces are in place. Off the genus-0 critical path
276	  % (the parent theorem `thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus`
277	  % is the only consumer in `RR.2`).
278	\end{proof}
279	
280	\begin{lemma}\leanok
281	[Iso-invariance of the Euler characteristic]
282	  \label{lem:euler_char_iso}
283	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_iso}
284	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
285	  \textit{Project-bespoke decomposition} (Archon-original packaging of
286	  the Mathlib \(\Ext\)-postcomposition API; no external source named).
287	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
288	  \(\bar k\) and let \(\mathcal F, \mathcal G\) be \(\Module \bar k\)-valued
289	  sheaves on \(C\) with an isomorphism \(\mathcal F \cong \mathcal G\).
290	  Then
291	  \[
292	    \chi(\mathcal F) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal G).
293	  \]
294	\end{lemma}
295	
296	\begin{proof}
297	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
298	  \leanok
299	  An isomorphism \(e \colon \mathcal F \xrightarrow{\;\cong\;} \mathcal G\)
300	  induces, in each cohomological degree \(n \in \mathbb N\), a
301	  \(\bar k\)-linear equivalence
302	  \[

\begin{lemma}[Global sections of the structure sheaf are one-dimensional]
  \label{lem:finrank_H0_toModuleKSheaf_eq_one}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.finrank_H0_toModuleKSheaf_eq_one}
  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule, def:Scheme_toModuleKSheaf}
  \textit{Specialisation of Hartshorne~I.3.4 (``\(H^0(X, \mathcal O_X) = k\)
  for any projective variety over an algebraically closed field'') to the
  project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured cohomology pipeline.}
  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
  \(\bar k\). The zeroth cohomology of the structure-sheaf carrier
  \(\toModuleKSheaf C\) is one-dimensional over \(\bar k\):
  \[
    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
    \;=\; \dim_{\bar k}\, \HModule\,\bar k\,(\toModuleKSheaf C)\,0
    \;=\; 1.
  \]
  The proof identifies \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)\) with the global-sections
  ring \(\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C) = \mathcal O_C(\top)\) through the
  project's \(\bar k\)-linear-equivalence chain
  (\cref{def:Scheme_HModule}). On the proper \(\bar k\)-scheme \(C\) this
  ring is a finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-algebra; integrality of \(C\)
  makes it a domain; and a finite \(\bar k\)-algebra that is an integral
  domain over the algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) is \(\bar k\)
  itself, so its \(\bar k\)-dimension is \(1\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  Proved directly in Lean.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[\(\chi\)-additivity across a short exact sequence with skyscraper cokernel]
  \label{lem:euler_char_of_shortExact_skyscraper}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_of_shortExact_skyscraper}
  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, lem:euler_char_shortExact_add,
        lem:euler_char_iso, lem:euler_char_skyscraperSheaf}
  \textit{Project-bespoke packaging of the Hartshorne~IV.1.3 inductive
  step, combining the three substrate lemmas above.}
  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
  \(\bar k\), let
  \[
    0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0
  \]
  be a short exact sequence of \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaves on
  \(C\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point such that the cokernel
  term \(\mathcal F_3\) is isomorphic to the skyscraper sheaf
  \(k(P) = \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\).
  Then
  \[
    \chi(\mathcal F_2) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal F_1) + 1.
  \]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:euler_char_shortExact_add, lem:euler_char_iso,
        lem:euler_char_skyscraperSheaf}
  Additivity of the Euler characteristic across the short exact sequence
  (\cref{lem:euler_char_shortExact_add}) gives
  \(\chi(\mathcal F_2) = \chi(\mathcal F_1) + \chi(\mathcal F_3)\).
  Iso-invariance of \(\chi\) (\cref{lem:euler_char_iso}) applied to the
  isomorphism \(\mathcal F_3 \cong k(P)\) rewrites the last term as
  \(\chi(k(P))\), and the skyscraper computation
  (\cref{lem:euler_char_skyscraperSheaf}) evaluates \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\).
  Combining the three identities yields
  \(\chi(\mathcal F_2) = \chi(\mathcal F_1) + 1\).
\end{proof}

\subsection*{Inductive scaffolding via the divisor line bundle}

The base case and the single-point inductive step of the
\(\chi\)-identity \cref{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus} are
pinned as three named Lean lemmas. Each references the line bundle
\(\mathcal O_C(D)\) of \texttt{RR.3} (\cref{def:sheafOf}) and so carries a
typed-sorry transitivity inherited from that sibling chapter; the
arithmetic scaffolding around \(\chi\) is, however, honest.

\begin{lemma}[Base case of the \(\chi\)-identity]
  \label{lem:euler_char_sheafOf_zero}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_zero}
  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, lem:finrank_H0_toModuleKSheaf_eq_one,
        lem:sheafOf_zero, def:genus}
  \textit{Base case of Hartshorne~IV.1.3 (the \(D = 0\) step).}
  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
  \(\bar k\). The Euler characteristic of the line bundle
  \(\mathcal O_C(0)\) of the zero divisor is
  \[
    \chi(\mathcal O_C(0)) \;=\; 1 - g(C).
  \]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:finrank_H0_toModuleKSheaf_eq_one, lem:sheafOf_zero, def:genus}
  The line bundle of the zero divisor is the structure-sheaf carrier,
  \(\mathcal O_C(0) = \toModuleKSheaf C\) (\cref{lem:sheafOf_zero}).
  Unfolding the two-term Euler characteristic
  \(\chi = \dim_{\bar k} H^0 - \dim_{\bar k} H^1\)
  (\cref{def:eulerChar_curve}) reduces the claim to
  \[
    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
    - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \;=\; 1 - g(C).
  \]
  The first term equals \(1\)
  (\cref{lem:finrank_H0_toModuleKSheaf_eq_one}), and the second term is by
  definition the genus, \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g(C)\)
  (\cref{def:genus}). Hence \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(0)) = 1 - g(C)\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Unit inductive step of the \(\chi\)-identity]
  \label{lem:euler_char_sheafOf_succ}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_succ}
  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, lem:euler_char_of_shortExact_skyscraper,
        lem:sheafOf_ses_single_add, def:sheafOf}
  \textit{Single-point inductive step of Hartshorne~IV.1.3 (the
  ``\(D \leftrightsquigarrow D + P\)'' step, additive direction).}
  Let \(C\) be as above, let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\), and let \(Y \in C\)
  be a prime (closed-point) divisor. Adding the single point \(Y\) raises
  the Euler characteristic by one:
  \[
    \chi(\mathcal O_C([Y] + D)) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + 1.
  \]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:euler_char_of_shortExact_skyscraper, lem:sheafOf_ses_single_add}
  The closed-point short exact sequence of \texttt{RR.3}
  (\cref{lem:sheafOf_ses_single_add}) provides a short exact sequence
  \[
    0 \to \mathcal O_C(D) \to \mathcal O_C([Y] + D) \to k(Y) \to 0
  \]
  of \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaves whose cokernel is the closed-point
  skyscraper \(k(Y)\). Applying the packaged \(\chi\)-additivity with
  skyscraper cokernel (\cref{lem:euler_char_of_shortExact_skyscraper}) to
  this sequence yields
  \(\chi(\mathcal O_C([Y] + D)) = \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + 1\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[General single-point inductive step of the \(\chi\)-identity]
  \label{lem:euler_char_sheafOf_single_add}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_single_add}
  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, lem:euler_char_sheafOf_succ, def:sheafOf}
  \textit{Iterated single-point step of Hartshorne~IV.1.3, both signs of
  the multiplicity.}
  Let \(C\) be as above, let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\), let \(Y \in C\) be
  a prime divisor, and let \(n \in \mathbb Z\). Then
  \[
    \chi(\mathcal O_C(n[Y] + D)) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + n.
  \]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:euler_char_sheafOf_succ}
  Induction on \(n \in \mathbb Z\). For \(n = 0\) the divisor \(n[Y]\)
  vanishes and the identity is trivial. For the positive direction,
  \((n+1)[Y] + D = [Y] + (n[Y] + D)\), so the unit step
  (\cref{lem:euler_char_sheafOf_succ}) and the inductive hypothesis give
  \[
    \chi(\mathcal O_C((n+1)[Y] + D))
    \;=\; \chi(\mathcal O_C(n[Y] + D)) + 1
    \;=\; \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + (n+1).
  \]
  The negative direction runs the same unit step backwards: applying
  \cref{lem:euler_char_sheafOf_succ} to the divisor \((n-1)[Y] + D\) and
  rearranging gives
  \(\chi(\mathcal O_C((n-1)[Y] + D)) = \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + (n-1)\),
  which is the inductive hypothesis transported one step in the negative
  direction.
\end{proof}
